falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
mechanism: Buyers keep placing more accelerator orders than Nvidia and its suppliers can build, so lead times and rental prices stay high.
rationale: A primary SEC filing shows a new six-year Nvidia deal for up to 40,000 GB300 GPUs, the vendor-financing program now spans about 210,000 GPUs across neoclouds, and providers are raising rental prices amid scarcity, so buyers keep ordering more than Nvidia can ship.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory and packaging supply catching up and to how much demand is propped up by Nvidia-extended financing.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
mechanism: Memory makers move capacity to HBM and hold back DRAM, so the memory and packaging bottleneck caps how many accelerators actually ship.
rationale: Three publishers report memory makers diverting wafer capacity to HBM and restricting standard DRAM, pushing AI-server rack costs up about 2% to 3% per week and 10% to 15% above baseline, which confirms memory as the binding limit on how many accelerators can ship.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory makers reversing the HBM capacity shift and restoring DRAM and packaging output.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: Buyers seeking a non-Nvidia option are turning to custom ASICs rather than AMD, so AMD's roadmap is not translating into datacenter share.
rationale: No finding this cycle shows an AMD hyperscaler win or share gain, and the only demonstrated non-Nvidia frontier path is Chinese custom ASICs rather than AMD Instinct, so the case that AMD turns its roadmap into material share softens; this reversal rests on two publishers with no AMD-primary evidence, so it is recorded but not yet applied.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to AMD landing a named hyperscaler Instinct deployment or a reported share gain.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: A frontier-scale model was trained entirely on custom AI ASICs instead of Nvidia GPUs, showing alternative silicon can take workloads that would otherwise buy merchant GPUs.
note: custom-asic-substitution: deferred: secondary-only reversal (2 distinct publishers < 3)
rationale: Two publishers report Meituan trained a 1.6-trillion-parameter frontier model entirely on more than 50,000 domestic non-Nvidia AI ASICs across 35 trillion tokens, showing custom silicon can absorb workloads that would otherwise buy merchant GPUs.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether custom-ASIC training stays an export-driven niche or spreads to Western hyperscalers at scale.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
mechanism: Providers are raising on-demand GPU rental prices as compute stays scarce, so rates hold or climb even as more capacity comes online.
rationale: Nebius raised on-demand GPU prices about 30% on June 1 and current listings hold H100 near $3.99 and B200 near $6.69 per GPU-hour, so rental levels are climbing rather than falling as capacity grows.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a wave of new capacity or memory-cost relief that lets providers cut rental rates.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: US and Taiwanese authorities are raiding and charging firms over unlicensed GPU shipments to China, keeping a large market legally off-limits.
rationale: Authorities escalated the smuggling case with raids on Supermicro's Taiwan office and employee detentions over about $2.5 billion of servers shipped to China without a license, reinforcing that enforcement keeps a large market off-limits, though only one publisher carried it this cycle.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a policy reversal that reopens licensed China sales at volume.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: Nvidia backstops purchases, shares revenue, and extends credit so capital-constrained neoclouds can buy its GPUs, so part of its reported demand rests on financing it provides itself.
rationale: A primary SEC filing plus reporting detail Nvidia's revenue-sharing and credit-support structure and an expanding backstop program covering roughly 210,000 GPUs across neoclouds, so more reported demand rests on financing Nvidia itself provides.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether financed neoclouds convert commitments into real cash revenue or default.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: Nvidia's demand is clustering into a small set of large and vendor-financed buyers, so a slowdown or default by any one of them would cut into reported demand.
rationale: The new deals cluster demand into a small set of vendor-financed neoclouds such as Firmus and Sharon AI, so a slowdown or default by any one of them would dent reported demand, keeping the concentration risk in place.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the buyer base broadens or stays clustered in a few names.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Customers keep buying complete Grace Blackwell systems that come with Nvidia networking, so the bundle raises the cost of switching to standalone accelerators.
rationale: No finding this cycle compares networking growth against compute, but customers keep buying complete Grace Blackwell systems that include Nvidia networking, so the systems bundle holds and the view is unchanged.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to open Ethernet alternatives winning networking sockets away from Nvidia.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Each Nvidia generation lowers the cost to run a token, so more inference workloads become economical to run on its GPUs.
rationale: No finding this cycle measures cost per token, and GPU compute demand stays strong enough for providers to raise prices, so the view that cheaper per-token cost widens the market is unchanged.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to rising memory and system costs eroding per-token gains or to a stall in generational efficiency.

falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Memory makers are diverting capacity to HBM to fill committed accelerator orders, so multi-year HBM commitments keep future demand in place.
rationale: Memory makers are diverting capacity to HBM to meet committed AI-accelerator orders and no finding reports any HBM contract cancellation, so the take-or-pay lock-in view holds.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to any HBM contract cancellation or renegotiation that would show the commitments are soft.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
mechanism: Aggressive enforcement against unlicensed China shipments keeps the legal, licensed channel narrow, so any revenue recovery there stays small.
rationale: Aggressive enforcement with raids and detentions over about $2.5 billion of unlicensed shipments keeps the legal China channel narrow, consistent with a small, capped recovery rather than a broad reopening.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a loosening of licensing or tariff terms that would lift the volume ceiling.

falsifiableTrigger: A funded compatibility layer or a rival software stack reaches parity such that two or more named hyperscalers move production inference off Nvidia within 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: A frontier model was trained entirely on non-Nvidia ASICs, showing teams can run large AI workloads without Nvidia's software stack when they must.
rationale: Two publishers report a 1.6-trillion-parameter frontier model trained entirely on non-Nvidia ASICs, direct evidence that large AI work can run off Nvidia's software stack, which softens the claim that software difficulty keeps customers locked in.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether non-CUDA training stays confined to export-restricted buyers or spreads to customers who could choose Nvidia.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost per S10 falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
detail.mechanism: Memory makers are moving capacity to high-margin HBM and holding back standard DRAM, so the memory portion of each AI-server bill of materials keeps rising.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to memory makers restoring DRAM output or HBM supply catching up to AI demand.
detail.statement: Fast-rising memory and NVMe prices are pushing up what it costs to build each AI server, which could strain buyer budgets and squeeze system margins.
detail.title: Rising memory costs inflate AI-server economics

note: prepaid-hbm-take-or-pay-locks-in-accelerator-demand: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 3 publisher domains)
conditional-china-re-opening-with-a-capped-ceiling: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 4 publisher domains)

falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
mechanism: NVIDIA's customers keep ordering GPUs faster than the company and its packaging suppliers can build and ship them, which keeps backlog high and lead times long.
rationale: Server supply-chain players report an AI order boom spilling into the second half of 2026, NVIDIA says it holds purchase orders from almost all major customers ahead of the Vera Rubin ramp, and Anthropic's Claude now runs in production on GB300 in Azure, so demand keeps pressing against supply even as capacity expands.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how quickly packaging and memory supply catch up to the order book.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
mechanism: The number of accelerators NVIDIA can actually sell is set by how much advanced packaging and stacked memory its suppliers can produce, not by wafer starts or order volume.
rationale: Suppliers are racing to add exactly the capacity the thesis names — ASE is boosting advanced packaging, Hanmi is moving into chip packaging, and NVIDIA has raised committed supply to $145 billion — which confirms packaging and stacked memory remain the binding lever, though this cycle's additions are early relief rather than a resolved bottleneck.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how fast new packaging and memory capacity from ASE, Hanmi, and others comes online.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: AMD is turning its Instinct roadmap into signed hyperscaler deployments and fast-growing data-center revenue, which builds a real second supply option to NVIDIA.
rationale: AMD landed a wave of named commitments — OpenAI as a core strategic compute partner from MI450, Meta's plan for up to 6 gigawatts of Instinct, and Oracle's MI355X racks — while data-center revenue grew 57% year over year to $5.8 billion, a clear improvement over last cycle even though Instinct still holds only about 5-7% of the accelerator market.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the OpenAI and Meta commitments convert into shipped revenue and rising share.

note: custom-asic-substitution: pending challenge lapsed (no same-direction confirmation)

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: When hyperscalers deploy their own chips such as Trainium and TPU, they buy fewer merchant GPUs, which would slow NVIDIA's and AMD's demand growth.
rationale: Although AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU are entering mass production, merchant GPUs still hold about 80% of the accelerator market and NVIDIA's order book is booming into the second half of 2026, so in-house silicon is not visibly displacing merchant-GPU demand growth this cycle.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether Trainium and TPU volumes grow faster than hyperscaler merchant-GPU orders.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
mechanism: Cloud providers keep renting NVIDIA GPUs at high hourly rates and buyers pay above list price for B200 hardware, so added capacity has not yet forced prices down.
rationale: B200 cloud rentals still sit at roughly $3.99 to $6.69 per GPU-hour, used B200 hardware trades near $45,000 above its $40,000 list, and NVIDIA holds a 75% gross margin, so prices are holding despite expanding supply; the lone soft data point, a $799 used H100 figure, is stale from early 2025 and not reliable.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether the wave of new accelerator supply in the second half of 2026 outpaces rental demand.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: U.S. export rules force AMD to license or halt China GPU shipments and add a 25% tariff, which cuts off addressable demand and triggers inventory write-downs.
rationale: Export rules cost AMD about $440 million in inventory and related charges in 2025, restrict MI308, require licenses for MI325, and force US inspection plus a 25% tariff on any China shipment, giving the risk a concrete price tag it lacked before.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to U.S. policy changes on China licensing and tariffs.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: NVIDIA shares revenue and extends credit so AI clouds can buy its chips, which means part of reported demand rests on financing NVIDIA itself provides rather than customer cash.
rationale: NVIDIA has launched a revenue-sharing and credit-support model that lets AI clouds procure its infrastructure, which is the exact mechanism the thesis warns about and shows vendor-extended credit increasingly underwriting reported demand.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how much booked demand relies on NVIDIA's own financing versus customer cash.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: A small set of large cloud buyers accounts for most accelerator revenue, so if one slows orders or shifts to in-house chips, demand drops sharply.
rationale: AMD's disclosure that a few customers drive most of its business shows accelerator revenue concentration is an industry-wide pattern, and NVIDIA still leans on a handful of large buyers; its new push to reach startups and regional players could broaden the base over time but has not yet changed the concentration picture.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether NVIDIA's reach into smaller and regional buyers actually widens its revenue base.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: NVIDIA sells its Spectrum-X and Quantum networking together with Blackwell GPUs, so customers who buy the compute also take the network, which raises the cost of switching to rival chips.
rationale: NVIDIA became the revenue leader in data-center Ethernet switching for the first time in Q1 2026 by bundling Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, confirming the systems-moat claim; as before, no finding compares networking growth to compute growth, so the sharper version stays unsupported.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether customers keep buying NVIDIA networking with the GPUs rather than mixing in standalone switches.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Each new NVIDIA generation does more work per chip and per watt, which lowers the cost of running each inference request and makes more workloads worth putting on its GPUs.
rationale: Blackwell Ultra (B300) delivers about 50% more compute and memory per chip than B200, continuing the generational efficiency gains that lower the cost of each inference request, and Anthropic now runs Claude in production on GB300 in Azure, an inference workload landing on NVIDIA hardware.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether each generation keeps cutting cost per token faster than rivals.

falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Multi-year, non-cancelable memory contracts and large upfront deposits commit buyers to pay for accelerator supply years ahead, so much of the planned buildout is contracted rather than speculative.
rationale: NVIDIA raised committed supply, including inventory purchase commitments and prepaids, to $145 billion and holds purchase orders from almost all major customers, showing multi-year commitments across the memory and accelerator chain are still growing rather than being cancelled.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to any cancellation or renegotiation of the memory take-or-pay commitments.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
mechanism: Regulators let AMD and NVIDIA ship some China chips only under licenses, inspections, and a 25% tariff, which caps how much revenue the China channel can recover.
rationale: The AMD disclosure matches the thesis exactly: China shipments of MI325-class chips are allowed only under licenses and US inspection and carry a 25% tariff, with MI308 restricted, keeping any China revenue recovery small and conditional.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether Washington keeps the tariff, inspections, and volume cap in place.

falsifiableTrigger: A funded compatibility layer or a rival software stack reaches parity such that two or more named hyperscalers move production inference off Nvidia within 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Rewriting AI software to run on non-NVIDIA chips is hard, so customers stay on NVIDIA even when AMD sells cheaper hardware.
rationale: OpenAI named AMD a core strategic compute partner from MI450, Meta committed to up to 6 gigawatts of Instinct, and MI350X is generally available on neoclouds running ROCm 7.0, showing major buyers adopting non-NVIDIA hardware despite the software switching cost; production inference has not yet clearly moved off NVIDIA, so the moat is eroding rather than broken.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how mature AMD's ROCm software becomes for large AI workloads.

falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost per S10 falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
mechanism: When DRAM and flash prices climb, each AI server costs more to build, which eats into system margins and can push buyers to trim orders.
rationale: No finding this cycle confirms fast-rising memory or NVMe prices, while ASE and Hanmi are adding packaging and HBM tooling capacity, which points toward easing memory supply rather than a price squeeze; the only mild support is that B300 carries about 50% more memory per chip, raising per-server memory content.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to the direction of DRAM and HBM prices as new memory and packaging capacity comes online.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Combined accelerator output across NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google fails to rise for 2 consecutive quarters, or packaging capacity per S10 stops expanding.
detail.mechanism: When several vendors bring next-generation chips to volume at the same time and packaging capacity grows, the total number of accelerators buyers can get rises.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether packaging and memory capacity keep pace with the multi-vendor ramp.
detail.statement: A simultaneous mass-production ramp of NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google accelerators is set to expand total accelerator supply in the second half of 2026.
detail.title: Multi-vendor accelerator supply wave in second-half 2026

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Any one of the named AMD mega-deals with Meta, OpenAI, or Oracle slips its deployment schedule by more than 1 quarter, or AMD Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: AMD's accelerator revenue rests on a few giant deployments, so if one partner delays or cancels, its data-center growth stalls.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to the timing and follow-through of the Meta and OpenAI commitments.
detail.statement: AMD's data-center accelerator growth depends on a small set of very large commitments, which concentrates both its upside and its downside in a handful of partners.
detail.title: AMD growth leans on a few mega-deals

note: cuda-software-lock-in-sustains-the-moat: promoted to registered (2 distinct asOfs, 6 publisher domains)

falsifiableTrigger: rpoBacklog stops growing while leadTimes fall below 26 weeks for 2 consecutive quarters as Data Center revenue keeps rising.
mechanism: AI buildout orders keep arriving faster than packaging and memory capacity can be turned into finished GPUs, so backlog and lead times stay stretched.
rationale: Server supply-chain players report an AI infrastructure order boom running into the second half of 2026, NVIDIA has piled up $119.0 billion of supply commitments to chase that demand, and new production wins like Anthropic on GB300 keep landing, so demand still outruns what NVIDIA can ship even as the packaging shortage begins to ease.
sensitivity: The pace at which advanced-packaging capacity closes the supply-demand gap.

falsifiableTrigger: CoWoS leadTimes fall below 26 weeks and HBM per S10 returns to available supply for 2 consecutive quarters while demand stays positive.
mechanism: Because advanced packaging and high-bandwidth memory are the scarcest inputs, the number of accelerators that can ship each quarter tracks packaging and memory capacity rather than wafer starts or order volume.
rationale: Advanced packaging is still the input that caps shipments, but it is loosening: the advanced (chip-on-wafer-on-substrate) packaging supply-demand gap is projected to narrow from about 20% to about 10% by end-2026, TSMC and its partners are lifting capacity toward roughly 200,000 wafers a month, more assembly-and-test firms are adding packaging lines, and NVIDIA says it has secured capacity beyond the next several quarters, so the cap on shippable GPUs is loosening.
sensitivity: How fast advanced-packaging and high-bandwidth-memory additions close the supply-demand gap.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Data Center revenue per D2 grows below 20% YoY with no new named hyperscaler deployment for 2 consecutive quarters, or Instinct market-share-pct stalls below 6%.
mechanism: Large named hyperscaler commitments plus a competitive MI450 and Helios roadmap turn AMD into a real second supply source, pulling orders and share into a market that used to have one vendor.
rationale: AMD stacked up major new deployments this cycle - up to 6 gigawatts with Meta, OpenAI as a core compute partner, Oracle, and a Rackspace agreement - and grew data-center revenue 57% year over year, so the roadmap is converting into real orders even though its accelerator share is still only about 5-7%.
sensitivity: Whether the signed Meta, OpenAI, Oracle, and Rackspace commitments convert to shipped, revenue-generating deployments on schedule.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA Data Center revenue per D2 grows above 20% YoY for 2 consecutive quarters while GPUs hold above 50% of hyperscaler build-out per market-share-pct.
mechanism: Hyperscalers deploying their own accelerators by the million meet a rising share of compute needs in-house, so custom chips capture a growing slice of buildout that would otherwise go to merchant GPUs.
note: custom-asic-substitution: deferred: secondary-only reversal (2 distinct publishers < 3)
rationale: The evidence now points the opposite way from prior cycles: custom-silicon shipments are projected to grow about 44.6% in 2026 versus 16.1% for merchant GPUs and to reach roughly 27.8% of the market, Anthropic has committed to up to one million Google chips, AWS has deployed over one million of its own, and Broadcom carries a $73 billion custom-chip backlog, so in-house silicon is taking a growing share of buildout growth; this is a reversal of the prior weakening whose supporting findings span two publishers rather than three, so it is recorded honestly even where downstream rules may hold off on fully applying it.
sensitivity: Whether custom-silicon shipment growth keeps outpacing merchant-GPU growth.

falsifiableTrigger: D6 H100 median falls below $2.50 per GPU-hour for 2 consecutive quarters, or B200 rental declines at least 15% at two or more providers within one quarter.
mechanism: Strong utilization keeps every available GPU busy, so added supply gets absorbed at existing rental rates instead of forcing price cuts.
rationale: Rental rates are holding: Lambda lists the Blackwell B200 at $6.69 an hour and the H100 at $4.29 an hour, both far above the $2.50 stress level, and the only cheap print is a used-H100 eBay figure that is stale from early 2025, so added supply has not yet forced discounting.
sensitivity: Whether the second-half 2026 multi-vendor supply ramp outpaces demand and forces rental discounting.

falsifiableTrigger: Commerce removes H200 China case-by-case licensing and the 25% tariff and NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: U.S. rules on selling advanced accelerators to China remove a large market from NVIDIA and AMD and force inventory write-downs, directly shrinking addressable demand.
rationale: Fresh disclosures deepen the constraint: NVIDIA's China revenue roughly halved to $4,550 million and it is effectively shut out of China data-center compute, while AMD took about $440 million of China-related inventory charges and faces licensing plus a 25% tariff, so policy is directly removing addressable demand.
sensitivity: U.S.-China policy shifts that reopen or further close China accelerator sales.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA's 10-Q shows customer financing or notes receivable rising for 2 consecutive quarters, or a named financed neocloud customer fails to convert its financed commitment to cash revenue within 1 quarter.
mechanism: When NVIDIA finances customers' purchases of its own hardware through revenue-sharing and credit support, part of reported demand rests on credit NVIDIA itself extends, so a customer stumble feeds back onto NVIDIA.
rationale: NVIDIA has now launched an explicit revenue-sharing and credit-support model that helps AI clouds procure its infrastructure, which is the exact financing channel the thesis warns about, so more reported demand is being underwritten by NVIDIA's own credit.
sensitivity: How much of NVIDIA's demand rests on credit it extends versus customers' own cash.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA top-3 direct-customer concentration falls below 40% of total revenue for 2 consecutive quarters per customerConcentration disclosure.
mechanism: NVIDIA's data-center sales lean on a handful of hyperscalers, so if one cuts orders or moves to in-house silicon, a large block of revenue is exposed at once.
rationale: A high-confidence SEC filing puts NVIDIA's top three direct customers at 21%, 17%, and 16% of revenue - about 54% combined and far above the 40% break level - and AMD separately reports the same reliance on a few customers, so the concentration risk is confirmed at a high level.
sensitivity: Whether any one of the top three customers slows orders or moves to in-house silicon.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA networking revenue grows slower than Data Center compute revenue for 2 consecutive quarters, or networking falls below 15% of Data Center revenue per market-share-pct.
mechanism: By pairing its Spectrum-X and Quantum networking with Blackwell and GB300 systems, NVIDIA raises the cost of swapping its GPUs for standalone accelerators, deepening a full-system lock-in.
rationale: NVIDIA took the top revenue spot in data-center Ethernet switching for the first time in early 2026 by bundling its Spectrum-X and Quantum networking with Blackwell and GB300, which supports the bundled-moat claim, though no finding compares networking growth with compute growth so the sharper version stays unproven.
sensitivity: Whether customers accept open, standalone networking instead of NVIDIA's bundled stack.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA cost per token fails to improve for 2 consecutive product cycles, or Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Each new NVIDIA generation does more work per watt, which lowers the cost of running a model and pulls more inference workloads onto its GPUs.
rationale: A new efficiency data point - the B200 delivers about 1.6 times the work per watt of the H100 - and Anthropic running production inference on GB300 both support the idea that cheaper-per-token compute pulls more inference onto NVIDIA GPUs, though the measure is efficiency rather than a direct cost-per-token figure.
sensitivity: Whether each new generation keeps cutting the cost of running inference.

falsifiableTrigger: Micron or its memory peers report cancellations or renegotiations of these take-or-pay agreements, or rpoBacklog tied to the deposits falls for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: Because large accelerator supply commitments are non-cancelable and backed by upfront money, buyers are contractually bound to take the capacity, so today's buildout is committed rather than easily reversed.
rationale: NVIDIA's $119.0 billion of non-cancelable supply commitments show large, locked-in accelerator supply obligations remain in place, and no memory supplier reported cancellations this cycle, so committed buildout continues to look contracted rather than speculative, though this cycle lacks a direct customer-deposit figure.
sensitivity: Whether any memory supplier reports a cancellation or renegotiation of a take-or-pay contract.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA books over $2B China Data Center compute revenue per exportControlExposure for 2 consecutive quarters despite the tariff and volume cap.
mechanism: Even where a license path exists, tariffs, security inspections, and volume caps make China accelerator sales uneconomic, so China revenue stays near zero.
rationale: ADJUSTED: China data-center compute is effectively closed to NVIDIA after its China revenue roughly halved to $4,550 million, and the only remaining path is narrow, license-by-license sales of AMD MI325-class chips carrying a 25% tariff and U.S. inspection, so any China revenue recovery stays minimal.
sensitivity: Whether Washington eases tariffs, inspections, or volume caps on China accelerator sales.

falsifiableTrigger: A funded compatibility layer or a rival software stack reaches parity such that two or more named hyperscalers move production inference off Nvidia within 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: As AMD's ROCm software and hyperscaler chips mature, large customers can run production workloads on non-NVIDIA hardware, so the software barrier that once locked them in is weakening.
rationale: The software moat keeps eroding: OpenAI, Meta, and Oracle are adopting AMD Instinct, AMD's MI350X is generally available on neoclouds running its ROCm 7.0 software, and hyperscalers are scaling their own chips, so non-NVIDIA hardware is increasingly usable for production even if NVIDIA still holds most of the market.
sensitivity: How close AMD's ROCm and hyperscaler software stacks get to matching NVIDIA's for production workloads.

falsifiableTrigger: AI-server memory bill-of-materials cost per S10 falls back toward baseline for 2 consecutive quarters, or DRAM output restrictions ease.
mechanism: If memory and storage prices climb faster than the rest of an AI server's parts, buyers either pay more per server or vendors absorb the cost and lose margin.
rationale: There is no fresh evidence of rising memory costs this cycle, and the opposite signal appears: AMD's gross margin rose to 55% with 56% guided, and Intel's new inference chip uses standard memory to dodge memory contention, so the claim that memory prices are squeezing server economics is losing support.
sensitivity: The direction of memory and storage prices relative to overall server cost.

falsifiableTrigger: Combined accelerator output across NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google fails to rise for 2 consecutive quarters, or packaging capacity per S10 stops expanding.
mechanism: NVIDIA, AMD, AWS, and Google are all ramping new accelerators to mass production at once, and expanding advanced-packaging capacity lets those ramps add up to more total supply in the second half of 2026.
rationale: The findings directly confirm the wave: NVIDIA Vera Rubin, AMD Helios, AWS Trainium 3, and Google chips are all moving into mass production at once, AMD's MI450 hits production in the second half of 2026, and expanding packaging capacity gives the ramps room, so total accelerator supply is set to rise into late 2026.
sensitivity: Whether advanced-packaging capacity keeps expanding fast enough to support all four ramps at once.

falsifiableTrigger: Any one of the named AMD mega-deals with Meta, OpenAI, or Oracle slips its deployment schedule by more than 1 quarter, or AMD Data Center revenue per D2 growth turns negative for 2 consecutive quarters.
mechanism: A few very large commitments from Meta, OpenAI, and Oracle drive most of AMD's accelerator growth, so the timing of those specific deals sets both its upside and its risk.
rationale: AMD's growth is visibly riding a few flagship commitments - up to 6 gigawatts with Meta, OpenAI as a core partner, Oracle, and a Rackspace deal - and AMD itself discloses reliance on a small number of customers, so its upside and downside both sit with a handful of partners.
sensitivity: Whether any single flagship customer delays or shrinks its committed deployment.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Intel cancels or delays Crescent Island past its second-half 2026 sampling target, or no named cloud provider adopts an LPDDR-based inference GPU within 2 consecutive quarters.
detail.mechanism: By using standard LPDDR5X memory instead of high-bandwidth memory, an inference chip avoids the advanced-packaging and memory queues that limit other accelerators, so it can be built with capacity that is not fought over.
detail.sensitivity: Whether memory-based inference chips can deliver competitive performance without high-bandwidth memory.
detail.statement: Intel's Crescent Island inference GPU uses standard LPDDR5X memory instead of high-bandwidth memory to sidestep the advanced-packaging and memory bottleneck, opening an alternative supply route for inference chips.
detail.title: Memory-based inference chips route around the packaging bottleneck

falsifiableTrigger: Two consecutive quarters where NVIDIA datacenter revenue (D2) falls sequentially while stated lead times shorten.
mechanism: NVIDIA keeps setting datacenter revenue records and filling its order book quarters ahead while memory stays sold out, so buyers still cannot get all the accelerators they want.
rationale: NVIDIA set another datacenter revenue record at $75.2B up 92% YoY, guided the next quarter to $91.0B, cited ~$20B visibility with purchase orders from almost all major customers ahead of Vera Rubin, while HBM stays sold out and gates availability. A fresh doubt about AI-infrastructure ROI and a 67% capex jump appears but has not yet dented orders, so demand still outruns supply.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to hyperscaler capex holding up; a sustained cut to the $650B 2026 capex plan would close the demand-over-supply gap.

falsifiableTrigger: TSMC CoWoS supply-demand gap (leadTimes) widening back above 20% or HBM order books (S10) clearing before 2028.
mechanism: Packaging capacity is expanding faster than memory, so the ceiling on shippable accelerators now sits on the memory line rather than the packaging line.
rationale: ADJUSTED: High-bandwidth memory, not advanced packaging, is now the supply that caps how much merchant-GPU revenue vendors can book. Packaging is easing as the TSMC CoWoS gap narrows from about 20% to 10% by end 2026 and ASE and Hanmi add capacity, and NVIDIA's supply commitments run to $119.0B-$145B, while HBM3E/HBM4 stay sold out through 2027 with demand far above supply.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to HBM3E/HBM4 yield and the pace of the 16-Hi HBM4 ramp scheduled for Q4 2026.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD Instinct share of the AI accelerator market (market-share-pct) failing to rise above 7% through 2026, or AMD datacenter revenue (D2) growth stalling for two quarters.
mechanism: AMD's datacenter revenue keeps growing and it is signing multi-year Instinct deployments, but its accelerator share is still only a mid-single-digit slice next to NVIDIA.
rationale: AMD datacenter revenue reached $5.8B up 57% YoY with a Q2 guide near $11.2B, it signed a phased Rackspace Instinct deployment, MI450 moves to production in the second half of 2026, and Cantor lifted its target to $700. But AMD Instinct is still only about 5-7% of the AI accelerator market versus NVIDIA around 80%, so momentum continues without a share breakout.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to MI450/Helios production execution in the second half of 2026 and ROCm software maturity.

falsifiableTrigger: Custom-ASIC share of AI-chip shipments (market-share-pct) stalling below 27.8% or its 44.6% growth rate falling under merchant-GPU growth in 2026.
mechanism: Custom accelerators are growing far faster than merchant GPUs and taking a rising share of AI-chip shipments, which trims the growth that would otherwise flow to merchant GPUs.
rationale: Fresh figures put custom ASICs at 27.8% of AI-chip shipments growing 44.6% versus 16.1% for merchant GPUs, with Broadcom's $73B backlog and Anthropic's 2nm Samsung talks reinforcing the shift, which resolves the pending challenge toward the thesis. Supporting evidence spans only two publishers (tomshardware, techcrunch), so under anti-whipsaw the reversal from weakened is recorded but not applied, and the counter that GPUs remain ~60% of even AWS build-out while custom silicon stays captive keeps conviction bounded.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether custom silicon stays captive; if it remains internal-only with no external rental, merchant-GPU demand growth is little displaced in practice.

falsifiableTrigger: H100 rental $/GPU-hr (D6) or secondary resale (gpuSpotPrice) falling further quarter over quarter as Blackwell supply ramps.
mechanism: As Blackwell supply arrives, clouds are cutting older-generation H100 and H200 rental prices sharply, so rental levels are no longer holding up across the board.
note: pricing-power-persistence: applied: corroborated secondary reversal (3 distinct publishers; pending filing checkpoint)
rationale: Major clouds cut H100/H200/A100 rental prices up to 45% with H100 falling from peaks near $8/hr, and Blackwell availability is expected to push another 10-20% down on H100 secondary pricing. Newer parts like B300 at $7.39/GPU-hr still hold, but prior-generation levels are clearly no longer holding across the board.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to Blackwell ramp speed, which sets how fast prior-generation prices decay.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA China revenue (exportControlExposure) recovering above $9,000M in a single quarter, signaling the constraint no longer binds.
mechanism: Export rules erased NVIDIA's China Hopper sales and halved China revenue this cycle even as Washington shifts H200-class licensing from denial to case-by-case.
rationale: Export rules erased NVIDIA's China datacenter Hopper sales versus $4.6B a year earlier and halved China plus Hong Kong revenue to $4,550M, AMD took ~$440M in charges, and Taiwan is escalating smuggling probes. A shift of H200-class licensing from denial to case-by-case and Chinese buyers signaling purchases points the other way, so the constraint stays material but not more so.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how many case-by-case H200 licenses actually convert to shipments versus staying at zero revenue.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA vendor-financed GPU commitments (rpoBacklog) climbing beyond the ~210,000 units across Firmus and Sharon AI in coming quarters.
mechanism: NVIDIA now guarantees revenue and buys back unused capacity for neoclouds that in turn buy its chips, so a growing slice of its demand is financed by NVIDIA itself.
rationale: NVIDIA launched a revenue-sharing and credit-support model, scaled CFO-approved vendor financing and rent-back guarantees across about 210,000 GPUs at Firmus and Sharon AI following CoreWeave $6.3B and Lambda $1.5B, and sold $25B of bonds, with multiple publishers flagging the circular-financing risk. This adds broad new corroboration that NVIDIA increasingly finances its own demand.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to neocloud creditworthiness; a single financed neocloud default would expose the circular loop.

falsifiableTrigger: Top-customer share of NVIDIA revenue (customerConcentration) falling below the prior 12/11/11 pattern, or the top three dropping under 21/17/16 percent.
mechanism: A handful of direct customers now buy over half of NVIDIA's revenue and their shares are rising, and vendor financing steers even more demand into a few neoclouds.
rationale: NVIDIA's Q1 filing shows three direct customers at 21%, 17% and 16% of revenue, and the annual filing shows one customer at 22% and another at 14%, up from a prior 12/11/11 pattern with the four largest more than half. Vendor financing further steers demand into a few neoclouds, so concentration is rising on fresh disclosure.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to any broadening of the buyer base beyond the current top four.

falsifiableTrigger: NVIDIA losing its number-one datacenter Ethernet switch revenue rank (market-share-pct) within a year, or Spectrum-X attach to GPU sales falling quarter over quarter.
mechanism: NVIDIA became the revenue leader in datacenter Ethernet switching for the first time and bundles Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300, tying networking to the GPU sale.
rationale: NVIDIA became the revenue leader in the global datacenter Ethernet switch market for the first time in Q1 2026, bundling Spectrum-X and Quantum with Blackwell and GB300. Taking the top switch rank while bundling is fresh, direct evidence that networking is deepening the full-system tie to the GPU sale.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to hyperscalers choosing merchant switching silicon over NVIDIA's bundled fabric.

falsifiableTrigger: A new NVIDIA generation failing to lower cost per token (flopsPerDollar), or AMD's per-dollar hardware edge translating into inference share gains within a year.
mechanism: Each NVIDIA generation cuts the cost per token, which makes more inference workloads worth running on its GPUs and enlarges the served market.
rationale: NVIDIA cites Grace Blackwell with NVLink delivering order-of-magnitude lower cost per token and says Vera Rubin extends the lead. AMD's MI300X shows a ~1.6x raw per-dollar edge in FP8, but that is hardware cost not system cost per token, so NVIDIA's each-generation cost-per-token drop still supports the demand pull.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to competitors matching cost-per-token at the system level, where AMD already shows a raw per-dollar edge.

falsifiableTrigger: Any of the 16 take-or-pay HBM contracts (rpoBacklog) being cancelled or deposits refunded within the 5-year term.
mechanism: Micron signed 16 non-cancelable five-year HBM contracts backed by more than $22B and about $18B of cash deposits, which pre-commits the memory that accelerators need and thus their future demand.
rationale: Micron signed 16 non-cancelable five-year take-or-pay HBM agreements backed by more than $22B with about $18B of cash deposits, and DRAM/NAND and HBM stay sold out through 2027 with order books into 2028. The scale of deposits is concrete new evidence that non-cancelable memory contracts pre-commit future accelerator demand.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to a demand air-pocket that would force buyers to forfeit deposits rather than take delivery.

falsifiableTrigger: Approved China licenses (exportControlExposure) lifting China revenue above the sub-50% cap, or the case-by-case window reverting to denial within two quarters.
mechanism: US policy now allows narrow, capped H200- and MI325-class licenses, so China reopens by exception rather than staying fully closed.
rationale: ADJUSTED: China datacenter compute is reopening to NVIDIA only through case-by-case H200- and MI325-class licenses capped under half of comparable US sales, with Chinese buyers now signaling first purchases. This cycle adds H200-class licensing shifted from denial to case-by-case and buyer interest from Alibaba, ByteDance and DeepSeek, broadening the earlier MI325-only picture, while China revenue sits at $4,550M and H200 licenses have booked no revenue yet.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether signaled Chinese H200 purchases actually clear licensing and generate revenue.

falsifiableTrigger: Additional frontier-scale models (S9) trained on non-NVIDIA accelerators within a year, or AMD ROCm design wins (releaseCadence) accelerating.
mechanism: A Chinese team trained a 1.6-trillion-parameter frontier model entirely on domestic non-NVIDIA chips, showing serious workloads can now run off the CUDA stack.
rationale: A Chinese team pretrained the 1.6-trillion-parameter LongCat-2.0 entirely on more than 50,000 domestic non-NVIDIA accelerators across 35T tokens, and AMD's MI350X is generally available on neocloud ROCm 7.0 with a raw per-dollar hardware edge. A frontier-scale model trained off the CUDA stack undercuts the claim that software difficulty keeps customers locked to NVIDIA.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to how portable mainstream training and inference software becomes across ROCm and custom stacks.

falsifiableTrigger: AI-server component cost inflation (S10) cooling below 1% per week, or DRAM/NAND pricing easing before 2027.
mechanism: AI-server rack component costs are climbing about 2-3% per week on memory and NVMe scarcity, directly raising the bill to build each server.
rationale: AI-server rack component costs are inflating about 2-3% per week, run 10-15% above baseline with up to a 40% weekly swing on memory and NVMe scarcity, and DRAM/NAND stay tight beyond 2027, direct support after last cycle's weakened read. This reverses direction on only two publishers (finance-biggo, www-investing), so under anti-whipsaw the move is recorded but not fully applied.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to when memory and NVMe supply loosens.

falsifiableTrigger: Any of Vera Rubin, MI450/Helios, Trainium 3 or Google TPU (releaseCadence) slipping mass production past 2H 2026, or HBM (leadTimes) throttling the combined ramp.
mechanism: NVIDIA, AMD, AWS and Google all reach mass production of next-generation accelerators in the second half of 2026, adding units, though shared memory scarcity caps how much total supply actually grows.
rationale: NVIDIA Vera Rubin, AMD Helios/MI450, AWS Trainium 3 and Google TPU are cited reaching mass production together in the second half of 2026, with Vera Rubin ramping Q3 2026 and MI450 in production in the second half. But HBM stays sold out through 2027 and gates GPU availability, so the simultaneous ramp is confirmed while the net supply expansion is capped.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to shared HBM and CoWoS supply, which all four vendors draw from.

falsifiableTrigger: AMD customer concentration (customerConcentration) easing to a broad base, or one large deal cancellation cutting datacenter revenue (D2) in a quarter.
mechanism: AMD says it depends on a small number of customers and its recent growth rides big commitments like the Rackspace 30 MW Instinct deployment, so a few contracts drive the trajectory.
rationale: AMD itself discloses it depends on a small number of customers and expects continued concentration, and its recent wins ride big commitments like the phased 30 MW Rackspace Instinct deployment, with analyst upside pinned to the MI450/Helios trajectory. AMD's own concentration disclosure is fresh, direct support that a few contracts drive its datacenter growth.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether MI450/Helios wins broaden beyond a handful of anchor customers.

falsifiableTrigger: Crescent Island (S9) missing its 2H 2026 sampling, or its LPDDR5X design still requiring the same advanced packaging capacity as HBM parts.
mechanism: Intel's Crescent Island inference GPU uses LPDDR5X instead of HBM, so it avoids the packaging and HBM contention that gates HBM-based accelerators.
rationale: Intel's Crescent Island datacenter inference GPU, shown at Computex 2026, uses LPDDR5X up to 480GB instead of HBM and is stated to avoid packaging and HBM contention, with sampling in the second half of 2026. This founding finding directly supports the thesis that memory-based inference chips sidestep the packaging bottleneck.
sensitivity: Most sensitive to whether LPDDR5X bandwidth is enough for real inference workloads at scale.

detail.falsifiableTrigger: TSMC wafer or CoWoS capacity (S10) running slack, or foundry prices flat to down over two quarters despite steady AI orders.
detail.mechanism: When every category of AI silicon competes for the same wafers and packaging, scarcity lets the foundry set higher prices.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to how much CoWoS and advanced-node capacity comes online versus demand across all AI chip types.
detail.statement: AI demand spreading across GPUs, custom ASICs, networking and power chips fills TSMC's capacity across nodes and lets it raise prices.
detail.title: Broadening AI demand tightens TSMC capacity and pricing

detail.falsifiableTrigger: Hyperscaler AI capex (D2) growth reversing from the 67% jump toward $650B, or order cancellations appearing within two quarters.
detail.mechanism: If AI infrastructure fails to earn its keep, the buyers financing it will slow their spending and merchant-GPU orders will fall.
detail.sensitivity: Most sensitive to measured returns on deployed AI infrastructure over the next few quarters.
detail.statement: Hyperscalers' sharp jump in AI capital spending risks a pullback if returns disappoint, which would cut accelerator orders.
detail.title: Hyperscaler AI spending may outrun near-term returns
